title: Checks on the presidency
description: Practice your skills in this quiz on how the president's agenda can create tension and frequent confrontations with Congress.
topic_path: Who checks the power of the president?

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following may the Senate do to limit the president’s power?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Reject presidential nominees
Fire and replace presidential appointees
Declare executive orders unconstitutional
File a motion for impeachment

assessment_items.question_content:
**Appointments to the president’s cabinet are sometimes contentious for which of the following reasons?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Cabinet-level officials are responsible for implementing the laws created by Congress, while still advocating for the president’s agenda
Cabinet-level officials are responsible for implementing Supreme Court decisions, while still advocating for the president’s agenda
Cabinet-level officials are responsible for presiding over the Senate and the House of Representatives to resolve tie votes on legislation
Cabinet-level officials are responsible for appointing and confirming White House staff and ambassadors to foreign countries

assessment_items.question_content:
**Appointments to the White House staff are often uncontested for which of the following reasons?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: These appointments do not require Senate confirmation
These appointments work primarily for the legislative branch
These appointments are only filled by former members of Congress
These appointments are nominated by the Speaker of the House

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the excerpt to answer the question below. 

>“Lifetime appointments and the new, highly ideological stakes provided senators ample incentives to use holds and silent filibusters to prevent a majority of their colleagues from acting on judicial nominations, both to block those with different ideologies and to keep slots vacant until the presidency moves into their party’s hands. Along the way, judicial confirmations have become increasingly politicized, and delays in confirming appellate judges have led to increased vacancy rates that have produced longer case-processing times and growing caseloads per judge on federal dockets.” 

>-Mann, Thomas E. and Norman J. Ornstein, *It’s Even Worse than it Looks*, 2012

**Which of the following statements best summarizes the authors’ argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Senate is more likely to place holds on life-tenured judicial appointments because of the lasting influence of those appointments
The Senate is less likely to place holds on life-tenured judicial appointments because these appointments have little influence
The Senate is more likely to place holds on judicial appointments than the House because its members represent more like-minded constituents
The Senate is less likely to place holds on judicial appointments when the majority party in the Senate differs from the president's party

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the excerpt to answer the question below. 

>“Lifetime appointments and the new, highly ideological stakes provided senators ample incentives to use holds and silent filibusters to prevent a majority of their colleagues from acting on judicial nominations, both to block those with different ideologies and to keep slots vacant until the presidency moves into their party’s hands. Along the way, judicial confirmations have become increasingly politicized, and delays in confirming appellate judges have led to increased vacancy rates that have produced longer case-processing times and growing caseloads per judge on federal dockets.” 

>-Mann, Thomas E. and Norman J. Ornstein, *It’s Even Worse than it Looks*, 2012

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the authors most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Senate confirmation serves as a check on appointment powers
Senate confirmation has no effect on judicial appointments
Senate confirmation has been streamlined in recent decades, resulting in faster appointments
Senate confirmation serves as an important check on the House of Representatives

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the excerpt to answer the question below. 

> “The defeat of Robert Bork’s 1987 Supreme Court nomination was a watershed event that unleashed what Stephen Carter has called ‘the confirmation mess.’ There was no question that Bork was a highly qualified nominee. He was rejected not because of any lack of qualification, or any impropriety, but because of his stated judicial philosophy: how he would vote as a judge.” 

> -John Maltese, “Anatomy of a Confirmation Mess: Recent Trends in the Federal Judicial Selection Process,” 2004


**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Judicial nominations can be contentious because of the lasting influence the people in those positions have after a president’s term
Judicial nominations have become more contentious because they reflect how little influence political philosophy has over the confirmation process
Judicial nominations are rarely challenged because the positions are usually filled with people who do not have any political leanings
Judicial nominations are contentious only if the sitting president and the majority of the Senate are of the same party

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b7beff0b3dfa7d9fd8b09e3a1edf723.png)

>Source: [ourpublicservice.org](https://ourpublicservice.org/issues/presidential-transition/political-appointee-tracker.php), Accessed June 1, 2018

**Which of the following is an accurate conclusion based on a comparison of the trends in the chart above and your knowledge of presidential appointments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The potential for conflict with the Senate depends upon the type of executive branch appointment
All appointments face the same level of scrutiny and are confirmed at about the same rate
Judicial appointments are the least contentious appointments because of their limited influence
Ambassador appointments are the most contentious appointments because of the delicate nature of diplomacy

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2583855bddb0e0d1c06cf5f680494f0c.png)

>Source: [Pew Research Center](http://www.pewresearch.org/fact-tank/2018/03/07/federal-judicial-picks-have-become-more-contentious-and-trumps-are-no-exception/)

**Which of the following statements is reflected in the data in the chart?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Presidents have become less successful at getting federal judges confirmed over the past 50 years
Presidents have more judicial appointments now than they did 50 years ago, leading to more failed confirmations
President Obama had more diverse appointments than any previous president, leading to more failed confirmations
Democratic presidents are less successful at having federal judges confirmed than Republican presidents

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The defeat of Robert Bork’s 1987 Supreme Court nomination was a watershed event that unleashed what Stephen Carter has called ‘the confirmation mess.’ There was no question that Bork was a highly qualified nominee. He was rejected not because of any lack of qualification, or any impropriety, but because of his stated judicial philosophy: how he would vote as a judge.” 

> -John Maltese, “Anatomy of a Confirmation Mess: Recent Trends in the Federal Judicial Selection Process,” 2004

**Which of the following statements best summarizes the author’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Robert Bork failed confirmation as a Supreme Court justice because some Senators were concerned about the impact he would have on the Court
Robert Bork failed confirmation as a Supreme Court justice because of several scandals during his nomination process
Robert Bork was successfully confirmed as a Supreme Court justice despite facing criticism for his ideological leanings
Robert Bork failed confirmation as a Supreme Court justice because he was an ethnic-racial minority appointee

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements explains how an executive order can cause conflict between the president and Congress?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An executive order enhances the power of the president because it create laws without congressional approval
An executive order enhances the power of the legislative branch because it creates laws the president cannot veto
An executive order enhances the power of the president’s cabinet because it creates laws without the president’s approval
An executive order enhances the power of Senate committees because it allows a simple majority vote to confirm presidential appointments

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements is the most likely motivation for a president to issue an executive order?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To achieve policy goals when a compromise cannot be reached with Congress
To force Congress to create policy that fits the president’s agenda
To create laws that cannot be changed or replaced over time
To increase the power of the presidency over presidential appointments

assessment_items.question_content:
>“Even as they welcome the opportunities opened up by having an ally in the White House, some worry that the continued emphasis on executive actions is just another step in the dilution of legislative power.

>“‘We need to go back to being the legislative branch,’ said Representative Ileana Ros-Lehtinen, a Florida Republican opposed to a potential executive order by Mr. Trump that would end a special program allowing younger illegal immigrants to remain in the United States. ‘We didn’t like this when Obama was doing it, so why should we accept it now?’”

> -Carl Hulse, “Trump Follows Obama’s Lead in Flexing Executive Muscle,” *The New York Times,* January 26, 2017

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would Representative Ros-Lehtinen most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Executive orders can create conflict with Congress as they do not require congressional input or approval
Executive orders are a valid use of legislative power and presidents should continue to use them in a divided government
Executive orders are unconstitutional and undermine Congress’s authority to create and pass legislation
Executive orders enhance legislative power for the majority party, limiting unnecessary debate in Congress

assessment_items.question_content:
**What best explains why the president's policy agenda may differ from the congressional agenda?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The president and Congress often represent mixed parties and constituencies.
The president and Congress are designed to represent opposing political parties and ideologies.
The president and Congress often represent the same interest groups and constituencies.
The Supreme Court ensures that congressional and executive policy agendas address national issues.

assessment_items.question_content:
A newly elected president has made immigration reform a top priority, focusing on pathways to citizenship and protections for undocumented immigrants brought to the United States as children. However, Congress is controlled by the opposing party, which has expressed strong resistance to comprehensive immigration legislation. Key committee chairs decline to hold hearings, and proposed bills stall before reaching the floor for debate. Despite public support for certain aspects of reform, partisan divisions in Congress create a legislative deadlock.

**What is the most likely response from the president to advance their policy agenda?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Issue an executive order directing immigration officials to stop enforcing regulations for certain undocumented immigrants
Vetoing any legislation from Congress until lawmakers agree to pass immigration reform measures supported by the administration
Bring a legal case to the Supreme Court and argue that congressional inaction on immigration violates constitutional obligations
Propose a constitutional amendment allowing the president to implement immigration laws without congressional approval in times of gridlock

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best explains why a president might face opposition from members of their own party in Congress when pursuing a legislative agenda?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Members of Congress must balance national party priorities with the interests of their local constituencies.
Members of Congress have limited formal power to influence legislation once the president proposes a bill.
Members of Congress are required to vote with their party on issues supported by the president.
The president can use the line-item veto to override congressional objections to specific parts of legislation.
